data.title: Boardwalk REIT: Look Past The Yield
data.description:
All values are in CAD unless noted otherwise.

Boardwalk Real Estate Investment Trust (TSX:BEI.UN:CA) operates primarily in the western provinces of Canada, namely Alberta and Saskatchewan.

Both the aforementioned provinces are not subject to rent control. That and the record-breaking immigration numbers have been the wind beneath this residential landlord's wings over the last few years. It has left other Canadian residential REITs on our radar, including the behemoth, Canadian Apartment Properties Real Estate Investment Trust (CAR.UN:CA), in the dust when we consider the total returns.

Boardwalk increased its monthly payout from 12 cents to 13.5 cents in March of this year. At the current price of $66.19, this REIT yields 2.45%, retaining its spot as the lowest yielder in the above list of residential landlords.

In comparison, CAR.UN yields 3.65%, MI.UN yields 3.85%, KMP.UN yields 4.08% and MRG.UN yields 4.40%. So if you came here for a high-yield choice, you have come to the wrong place.

Prior Coverage

Our coverage of the 2024 third quarter results was a mea culpa of sorts. We had underestimated the impact of the influx of immigrants and rated it a sell back in May 2023. Subsequently, the REIT soared and we watched the party from the sidelines. With the stock having retraced to the 2023 levels, we reversed course and slapped a buy rating on it. This was not as knee-jerk a reaction as the previous sentence makes it sound. There was an accompanying analysis of the fundamentals and it was laid out in that piece. Rather than an outright long position, we suggested slightly in the money, $60 strike covered calls expiring in December 2025. We personally executed the trade too for 800 shares.

With 4 months into that call, the stock is holding above the $60 mark, but we still have some time to go. While the position could be profitably closed at this time, personally, we plan to hold till expiration.

While we rated the REIT a buy then, there were some concerns about the headwinds from the impact of the slowing immigration and the accompanying rise in vacancies/lower market rents. The pros just outweighed the cons. Today we get into the fresh off the oven Q1-2025 results to see if the advantages of holding this REIT still eclipse the risks.

Q1-2025

The average occupancy declined slightly on a year-over-year basis from 98.83% to 97.84%. This decline was primarily from an increase in the supply of suites and a reduction in the migration numbers (Canada's immigration policy is full reverse mode) for Alberta and Saskatchewan. The higher year-over-year average rent ($1,538 versus $1,418), combined with declining incentives, more than offset the negative impact of the increase in vacancies. The overall rental revenue beat the comparative quarter by 7.2%.

The operating expenses also rose (2.5%), primarily due to higher utility costs. Boardwalk expects this category to moderate going forward due to the removal of the federal carbon tax effective April 1. The increase in expenses was easily absorbed by the rental inflows, and the net operating income (NOI) came in a solid 10.3% higher on a year-over-year basis.

The weighted average interest rate on mortgages increased from 3.02% in Q1-2024 to 3.19% in Q1-2025. While rates were cut by the Bank of Canada in 2024, Boardwalk's mortgages were so far below the CMHC rates, that refinancing pushed up the weighted average. Despite that, the impact on the FFO was marginal, as can be seen from the breakdown below.

Boardwalk has been buying back units under its NCIB program, and that has amplified the 11.1% overall FFO increase to an 11.6% increase on a per-unit basis.

The REIT is very positive about the subsequent quarters of this year and even revised its guidance to the upside.

The following excerpt from the financial report provides some color on the optimism.

The Trust anticipates outsized revenue and NOI growth in its largest market of Edmonton, as well as some of its more affordable non-price controlled markets on a year-over-year basis, as a result of strong performance year-to-date and ongoing positive blended leasing spreads throughout the remainder of 2025. The Trust is also incorporating the estimated impact of the removal of the federal carbon charge on utility costs for the balance of the year.

Source: Q1-2025 Financial Report

With the restrictive immigration policies and the higher suite supply, we expect the occupancy to drift down a bit further. Considering the buffer it has with the leasing spreads, Boardwalk should be OK overall.

Outlook & Verdict

Boardwalk marked up the value of its assets, with its NAV increasing from $93.68 at the end of last year to $96.07. This increase was driven by the NOI, with the capitalization rate [cap rate] remaining the same as December 31.

Since Boardwalk retains a substantial portion of their FFO, it also adds to NAV over time. The market is pricing Boardwalk at an implied cap rate of around 6.4%, whereas we are more in line with the REIT's assessment of its cap rate. You can see this to some extent in the REIT's transactions in the quarter. This is also supported by Colliers Canada Q4-2024 numbers for Edmonton and Calgary, the primary locations of this REIT's portfolio.

Boardwalk is trading at an FFO multiple of 14.51X.

While it is not exactly cheap in comparison to its peers (CAR.UN 16.55X, KMP.UN 14.23X, MI.UN 13.25X and MRG.UN 10.25X), it has delivered the numbers quarter after quarter. We will also argue that the entire group is cheap and worth buying and holding for the next decade. Also, the 14.51X FFO multiple is still inexpensive compared to its own history. The discount to NAV, while juicy, appears to be the norm for Boardwalk.

While that may sound pessimistic, we actually think this REIT will catch up tob its NAV in the future. Their performance over the last few years has been fantastic, and if this REIT does not deserve a premium to NAV, we are not sure who does. Note that the chart does not incorporate this morning's results and the actual discount to NAV is a bit wider than shown above.

We are happy with our investment in Boardwalk and based on the market action after the Q1 results, we are not alone with this sentiment. Since we already have a good sized position, we will not be adding more at this price. There are other beaten down REITs which have our attention at the moment, but we will definitely add to our position on a decline. Those that have stayed out can consider a starter position and average down if the price drops. This REIT remains a buy for us at this time, and we think it will deliver a 10%/year total return from here.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult with a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

data.title: Tariff Turmoil Shines Spotlight On S&P/ASX Geographic Revenue Exposure Indices
data.description:
By Sean Freer

With the surprisingly widespread U.S. tariff hikes announced by President Trump in early April, the geographic source of corporate revenues is coming under the spotlight, as reciprocal trade agreements are being redrawn globally.

The resulting volatility of share prices following Trump’s April 2 “Liberation Day” speech is not surprising, given the globalization and interconnectedness of company revenue sources and supply chains. Some companies are more exposed to a potential trade war than others; for example, those that have products or inputs to products that are highly reliant on foreign markets (such as miners and automakers), while other companies may be more domestically focused (such as banks, telecommunications and utilities companies).

The S&P/ASX 200 Geographic Revenue Exposure Indices offer insight into the performance of Australian companies. The S&P/ASX 200 Australia Revenue Exposure Index includes companies from the S&P/ASX 200 that have greater-than-average revenue exposure to Australia. Conversely, the S&P/ASX 200 Foreign Revenue Exposure Index includes the companies with greater-than-average exposure to markets outside Australia. The indices are weighted by float market cap.1

A Return to Domestic Outperforming

After a lengthy period of underperformance, the S&P/ASX 200 Australia Revenue Exposure Index has reverted to a strong period of outperformance over the past two years. More recently, the S&P/ASX 200 Australia Revenue Exposure Index has outperformed the S&P/ASX 200 Foreign Revenue Exposure Index in each of the first four calendar months of 2025, with April being a watershed month.

The recent strong performance numbers have resulted in the S&P/ASX 200 Australia Revenue Exposure Index outperforming within all the time periods up to the 5-year period ending April 30, 2025, whereas the S&P/ASX 200 Foreign Revenue Exposure Index outperformed over the 10-year period.

Performance Drivers

While trade policy has certainly influenced the short-term price movements, several other factors have influenced longer-term performance, including fluctuations in both currency and interest rates.

The types of companies and sectors represented in each index also help identify performance drivers. The S&P/ASX 200 Foreign Revenue Exposure Index is overweight in companies in the Materials, Health Care and Information Technology sectors relative to the S&P/ASX 200, while the S&P/ASX 200 Australia Revenue Exposure Index is tilted toward the Financials, Real Estate, Communication Services, Consumer Staples and Consumer Discretionary sectors.

Over periods beyond five years, the S&P/ASX 200 Foreign Revenue Exposure Index has outperformed on the back of strong returns within the aforementioned overweighted sectors; Materials, Health Care and Information Technology. Many companies in these sectors have benefitted from a weakening Australian dollar from 2013 to 2022.

However, more recently, a somewhat stabilized exchange rate and the interest rate increases in 2022 and 2023 have supported the large banks, while domestic consumer companies and those in the Real Estate sector have also performed well during the past two years despite cost-of-living concerns.

If we review rolling three-year annualized periods, we can see more clearly the phases in which companies with greater domestic revenue exposure outperformed and vice versa. Since the Australian dollar dropped below 80 cents to the U.S. dollar, the foreign revenue exposure index consistently outperformed the Australian revenue exposure index by more than 5% until about two years ago, when the rising interest rates supported Financials—a large component in the S&P/ASX 200 Australian Revenue Exposure Index.

The S&P/ASX Geographic Revenue Exposure Indices highlight how trade policy, currency movements and interest rates can have a meaningful impact on differing segments of the Australian equity market. The S&P/ASX 200 Geographic Revenue Exposure Indices group constituents as either majority foreign or domestic earners and are a useful lens to analyze market performance during different macro events or stages of an economic cycle.

1 Please refer to the methodology document for S&P Global Revenue Exposure Indices for more information.

Investment Approach

  • Fidelity Freedom® Funds (the Funds) are designed so that the target date referenced in the Fund name is the approximate year when investors expect to retire.
  • Except for Fidelity Freedom® Income Fund, each of the Funds seeks high total return until reaching its respective target retirement date; thereafter, each Fund's objective will be to seek high current income and, as a secondary objective, capital appreciation.
  • Except for Fidelity Freedom® Income Fund, each Fund's asset allocation strategy becomes increasingly diversified as it approaches its target date – and beyond. Ultimately, the Funds are expected to merge with Fidelity Freedom Income Fund.
  • The Funds employ a disciplined and time-tested investment process focused on helping investors achieve successful retirement outcomes by leveraging the depth and strength of Fidelity's investment research and resources.

To learn more or to obtain the most recent month-end or other share-class performance, visit fidelity.com/performance, institutional.fidelity.com, or 401k.com. Total returns are historical and include change in share value and reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any.

Cumulative total returns are reported as of the period indicated.

For definitions and other important information, please see the Definitions and Important Information section of this Fund Review.

Market Update and Portfolio Review

  • Uncertainty about the direction of U.S. policy weighed on markets in Q1, as investors digested news on tariffs, cuts to government programs, and tighter immigration policy. U.S. equities fared poorly, especially large-cap growth and smaller-caps. Tariffs were a catalyst for the repricing of non-U.S equities. Non-U.S. markets' fiscally oriented growth narrative, cheaper valuations, and improved earnings estimates drove one of the largest relative-return differences between U.S. and non-U.S. equities in history. A falling U.S. dollar also boosted non-U.S. stocks. Fixed-income assets served as an equity diversifier amid near-term risks related to economic growth, inflation and the path of interest rates.

Total Return of Strategic Asset Classes - Period Ending March 31, 2025

You cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

U.S. Equities - Dow Jones U.S. Total Stock Market Index, Non-U.S. Equities - MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (Net MA), U.S. Short-Term Inflation-Protected Bond - Bloomberg U.S. TIPS 0-5 Years Index, U.S. Long-Term Inflation-Protected Bond - Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Inflation Notes: 5+ Years Index, U.S. Investment-Grade Bond - Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, Long-Term U.S. Treasury Bond - Bloomberg U.S. Long Treasury Index, International Bond - Bloomberg Global Agg Treasury ex USD, ex EM, RIC Capped, Float Adjusted USD H Index, Short-Term Debt - Bloomberg U.S. 3-6 Month Treasury Bill Index
  • Fidelity's target date strategies produced positive absolute returns during Q1. Performance for the strategies as of March 31, 2025, is displayed below (net of fees)*.

Quarterly Performance for Representative Funds - Period Ending March 31, 2025

Performance Attribution Summary

  • Active asset allocation decisions and the performance of the underlying portfolio managers contributed to the fund's performance versus the composite index in Q1. In particular, an overweight in non-U.S. equities and underweight in U.S. equities added meaningful value to the fund's relative result.
  • Among the underlying portfolios, non-U.S. equity portfolio managers, in aggregate, notably contributed to the fund's relative performance, as did U.S. investment-grade bond managers.

Relative Contributors (representative across vintages):

U.S. and non-U.S. equity funds with a value-oriented investment philosophy contributed to the fund's relative result this quarter. The largest fund-level individual contributors:

  • Fidelity® Series Large Cap Stock Fund outperformed its benchmark, the S&P 500® index. The fund emphasizes companies for which long-term normalized earnings and free-cash-flow are underappreciated by investors. Sector allocations notably contributed to the fund's relative outcome, particularly an underweight in information technology. Overweight positions in GE Aerospace and Exxon Mobil (XOM) were among the fund's top individual relative contributors. Not owning benchmark stock Tesla (TSLA) also helped.
  • Fidelity® Series International Value Fund outpaced its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Value Index. The fund is managed by Alex Zavratsky, who believes that investing in attractively valued, high-quality companies (e. g., a strong balance sheet, stable return on equity and recurring cash flow) provides the best chance of outperformance in the long run. Outperformance was driven by security selection, primarily in the industrials sector, where an out-of-benchmark position in German auto and arms manufacturer Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF)(OTCPK:RNMBY) was the largest individual relative contributor. A non-benchmark stake in British-based defense and aerospace firm BAE Systems (OTCPK:BAESF)(OTCPK:BAESY) also helped, along with an overweight in Banco Santander (SAN).
  • Fidelity® Series Emerging Market Opportunities Fund outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. The fund is managed by a team of sector-based portfolio managers who specialize in security selection within their respective sectors. Security selection within the communication services, materials and health care sectors lifted the fund's relative result. Top individual relative contributors included an out-of-benchmark position in Singapore-based digital entertainment, e-commerce and digital finance conglomerate Sea, and overweight stakes in South African firm Impala Platinum Holdings (OTCQX:IMPUF)(OTCQX:IMPUY) and multinational commerce group PDD Holdings (PDD).

Relative Detractors (representative across vintages):

U.S. and non-U.S. equity portfolios with a growth bias generally detracted this quarter. The largest fund-level individual detractors:

  • Fidelity® Series Growth Company Fund underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 3000® Growth Index. The fund is managed by Steve Wymer, who invests across a spectrum of companies, from blue chip growth to aggressive growth. Steve focuses on firms operating in well-positioned industries and niches, and those he considers capable of delivering persistent sales and earnings growth. Security selection within the health care sector notably detracted from relative performance for the quarter. The largest individual relative detractors included a larger-than-index stake in footwear designer and distributor Deckers Outdoor (DECK), along with semiconductor companies Nvidia and Astera Labs.
  • Fidelity® Series Blue Chip Growth Fund trailed its benchmark, the Russell 1000® Growth Index. The fund is managed by Sonu Kalra, who invests in companies that he believes have above-average earnings-growth potential and a sustainable business model. Security selection within information technology, including overweights in Marvell Technology (MRVL), Astera Labs (ALAB) and Nvidia (NVDA), meaningfully detracted from performance. An underweight and security selection in the financials sector also hurt.

Active Asset Allocation:

Active asset allocation positioning had a positive influence on the fund's relative performance this quarter, especially an overweight in non-U.S. equities and an underweight in U.S. equities.

Outlook and Positioning

  • The fund's glide path and strategic asset allocation reflect our long-term views and insights on participant needs, diversification and capital markets. Our investment process focuses on selecting strategic asset classes that provide appealing long-term returns, independent sources of return and risk, and favorable portfolio implementation attributes.
  • We maintain the view that trends in the economy and policy conditions may imply a regime change for financial markets. High debt, aging demographics, peak globalization, and geopolitics continue to create uncertainty in the path of inflation, policy and corporate profits.
  • With this in mind, strategic asset allocation decisions continue to emphasize diversification to help us navigate different risks. In recent years, we have amplified portfolio diversification based on a view that participants will experience multiple market environments throughout their lifetime, and that regimes can change abruptly.
  • Over long periods, equities have been a powerful asset for building wealth, while our fixed-income portfolio provides balance during periods of inflationary and deflationary stress. The fund's active allocation positioning reflects intermediate-term views on assets that we believe are mispriced relative to our view of fair value.
  • We expect the U.S. economy to muddle through tariff-induced challenges because of its large, diversified, services-based economy, with resilient companies and consumer-related sectors. We also acknowledge the considerable uncertainty about the outlook for the domestic economy, as investors, consumers and businesses assess the likely duration and impact of policy changes.
  • While non-U.S. companies are facing the risk of weaker demand from U.S. consumers, a number of factors may buffer the impact, including lower starting valuations and earnings expectations, fiscal room for governments to offset the impact of tariffs, and the opportunity to repatriate capital that has flowed into U.S. assets over the past decade.
  • We believe the range of possible outcomes is wide and the landscape is changing rapidly. This has caused volatility in both equity and fixed-income markets. We continue to assess incoming information as it occurs, in collaboration with analysts across Fidelity's multi-asset, equity and fixed-income teams. We continue to evaluate the fund's positioning, while adhering to our diversified, long-term approach.
  • In our view, heightened volatility and the persistence of uncertainty in the capital markets reinforce the importance of a disciplined investment process. Our target-date strategies are managed with a long-term investment horizon and provide investors with a diversified portfolio that seeks to balance their need for total returns with their sensitivity to many risks – longevity, market, inflation and deflation. During the 29 years we have managed target-date funds, this approach has proven to be a prudent and time-tested strategy. Since inception of Fidelity's target-date strategies, we believe we have achieved successful outcomes, as measured by total returns and by investors' tendency to remain invested throughout periods of volatility. We remain focused on researching the key drivers of outcomes for retirement plan participants and retirement investors, including capital markets, diversification and future expectations of participant attributes.

data.title: V2X: Bidding Faster And Bigger, But Growth Does Not Inspire
data.description:
V2X (NYSE:VVX) reported its first quarter earnings on the 5th of May, beating analyst estimates on EPS, but missing estimates on revenues. The stock price dropped in response to the earnings release and is now down 4% since my prior coverage despite a buy rating. The stock price obviously has not developed in the direction I believe it should, but we also note that the S&P 500 dropped 5.6% since my last coverage.

In this report, I will discuss several elements that exert pressure on the stock price. After that, I will discuss the most recent earnings and update my price target for the stock.

Why Does V2X Stock Price Remains Challenged?

There are various reasons why V2X has some challenges to seeing its upside materialize. Taking a top-down approach, the first pressure the company faces is its ownership. In 2018, AIP acquired Vertex Aerospace from L3Harris Technologies (LHX) and AIP obtained a 62% stake in V2X after a reverse merger with Vectrus in 2022. Quite often, when a single party holds a significant share in a company, it does provide some stability, but it also limits upside as there always is the possibility that the major shareholder reduces its stake in the company, bringing more shares in circulation. That is what happened last year when AIP brought some shares to the market in two tranches. This reduced the ownership to 44.9% and exerted some pressure on the stock price.

Going a level deeper, there are some concerns regarding trade and the impact from DOGE. The push for efficiency within the US government has exerted pressure on US defense contractors and for V2X that has also been seen as a risk. However, I do believe that in many cases the push for a more efficient government may also cut costs that defense contractors currently carry to go through all procurement processes, paperwork for products and services and certification.

During the first quarter earnings call, the company also shared that it has not experienced any headwinds on trade or defense budgets:

There is nothing we have seen to date from tariffs, budgetary requests, administration priorities that would change our strong position in the markets we serve. Our visibility into future macroeconomic decisions, policy trends, national security and emerging defense budget priorities suggest positive impacts on our current work as well as favorable opportunities for our new business pipeline.

So, the impact, if any, seems to be limited, but that hasn’t stopped investors from selling at times. The fact V2X is not a market darling is partially also because of its margins. The company is struggling to expand its EBITDA margins significantly above 7%. The typical margins we are looking for in aerospace and defense companies are between 10-15%, and V2X is way below that. That means that in terms of margins there are more attractive businesses and if there is any pressure from tariffs, it would lower the margins even further. So, whereas other companies have some padding to absorb margin headwinds and still end up within an appreciable margin range, V2X does not. Furthermore, the first quarter sales growth was uninspiring.

V2X Sales Grow High Single Digits

First quarter sales barely showed any growth, and that does not help investor sentiment. Moreover, the $5 million increase in sales missed analyst estimates by $20 million. Sales growth was booked in the Pacific region during the quarter while the Warfighter Training Readiness Solutions [WTRS] program ramped up, while the KC-10 program and T-1A programs partially offset that growth. Sales in the US increased by 6% or $32.8 million, while sales in Asia grew $7.2 million or 10.4%. This was almost fully offset by declines in Europe and the Middle East. Important to note is that sales by geographic end market do not indicate the region of the customer, but the region where the contract is performed. This means that a portion of the declines is attributable to lower contract activity carried out in other regions but contracted by US customers.

Adjusted EBITDA declined by $2 million to $67 million. This is in line with the outline that the company will have a backloaded EBITDA generation, with 45% of the EBITDA generated in H1 and the remaining in the second half of the year. The company saw its adjusted operating cash flow decrease from an $84 million outflow to a $118 million outflow, driven by timing of customer payments and lower cash collections.

The EBITDA margins decreased from 6.8% to 6.6%, which is below the margins of 7.1% to 7.4% that are guided for the full year, but match the company’s backloaded profile.

So, first quarter results were characterized by low growth, worse cash out flow and lower margins. That is not a convincing cocktail.

More positive is the fact that the company is bidding on more opportunities, it is also submitting bids for bigger contracts and that could eventually become a driver of margin expansion if V2X is selected. Bigger contracts do not necessarily mean higher margins, but they give the company more time and dollars to amortize any headwinds. So, cost correction can happen over a larger base and will have a relatively lower impact on contract margins.

V2X Confirms Guidance

For the full year, the company continues to expect $4.375 billion to $4.5 billion in sales, which indicates 2.7% growth at the midpoint of the range. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be between $305 million and $320 million, indicating margins of 7%. EPS of $4.45 to $4.85 indicates 11.5% growth, but analysts have estimates EPS at $4.75. Adjusted net cash from operations is expected to be $160 million, compared to $161 million a year ago. Just like the first quarter results, it is rather low-growth and uninspiring.

V2X Is Low-Growth With Big Upside

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Nothing really seems to be inspiring about V2X. The company pays no dividend and EBITDA growth through 2027 is expected to be 4% with downward revision to the estimates in 2025 and 2026. These revisions were down 4.2% in 2025 and down 2.9% for 2026. On free cash flow, free cash flow estimates have come down for 2025 driven by strong cash collection in 2025, but 2026 estimates have come down by 11% driven by higher CapEx.

This does not seem like the recipe for a buy rating, but I do have a buy rating on the name for the simple reason that the stock is undervalued against the fundamentals and lowered projections. In the base case scenario, there is 33% upside to $65.26 and in a more bullish scenario with improved market sentiment, I do believe the stock price could rise 45% to $70.90.

Conclusion: Delivering Where It Matters

V2X is not really an inspiring name for investment with low single digit EBITDA growth, low EBITDA margins and it pays no dividends. However, from a fundamental perspective, the stock remains undervalued, likely driven by the significant ownership by AIP, which at times may trigger share price weakness when AIP divests part of its ownership. However, I do believe that the market currently significantly underappreciates V2X, providing a compelling investment opportunity.

data.title: Monroe Capital: Poor Investment Choice Despite The Massive 14% Dividend Yield
data.description:
Thesis

When a stock is trading near its 5-year lows and especially when it is in a long-term downtrend, it is obviously clear that something is wrong.

Even more so when that stock is a BDC offering a high 14% dividend yield, which under different circumstances would normally act as a magnet for long-term investors.

Monroe Capital (MRCC) is a small-cap BDC with a market capitalization of approximately $150 million and stands out as one of the most highly leveraged players in the sector.

The stock’s performance along with an analysis of Monroe’s financials justifies a “HOLD” recommendation, despite the generous $1 per share dividend.

The stock has weak prospects for recovery and growth, making it difficult to bet on outperformance. At the same time, dividend-focused investors have better options in the sector.

The Company yesterday announced its Q12025 results, which confirmed its weakness as adjusted Net Investment Income fell by 34% to $0.19 per share (below the $0.25 dividend), putting the Company’s only real advantage at risk.

Stock Overview

The behavior of the stock has been disappointing. Since its debut on the Nasdaq in late 2012 at $15 per share, it has gradually lost 50% of its value, now trading around $7, near lows that took place during the pandemic. The stock has never managed to give a meaningful upward movement, despite having long traded at a discount (currently at 0.83x NAV).

In the meantime, there is not any buying interest that could shift the narrative, despite the company distributing a generous and consistent annual dividend of $1 (paid quarterly).

This happens for two reasons. First, the company’s overall financial profile remains weak while Q4 2024 earnings release only confirmed those concerns. Second, while Monroe’s 14% yield is certainly attractive at current levels, similar (or slightly lower) yields can be found in much stronger names within the sector such as Trinity Capital (14%) and Capital Southwest (12.4%).

After all, dividend yield is only half the story. What truly matters is total return because while BDC investors may seek reliable income flows, they are not willing to lose in capital value what they gain from distributions.

MRCC has delivered a modest +51% total return over the past decade, an unacceptably poor performance, especially when compared to the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), which has returned roughly three times as much.

The Company's performance is even weaker when it is compared with other BDCs that, in my point of view, are far more attractive. For instance, Main Street Capital (MAIN, +279%), Fidus (FDUS, +251%), Ares Capital (ARCC, +230%), Hercules Capital (HTGC, +299%) and Gladstone Capital (GLAD, +289%).

You can, clearly, see the long-term downward trend on the chart, which was recently confirmed by MRCC’s failure to break above the $9 level (resistance). For now, the stock is trying to find support in the $6.80–$7.10 in order to avoid falling to new five-year lows below.

High leverage, weak net investment income outlook

The likelihood of continued downside increases if we accept the scenario of eventual Fed rate cuts, which could negatively impact net investment income -NII- by reducing cash flow generation. While the extent of this impact on NII remains relative, it is reasonable to expect medium-term concerns, particularly for BDCs like MRCC.

The more debt a BDC carries, the more exposed it is to risk. Monroe is actually one of the most heavily leveraged in the sector, with a debt-to-equity ratio over 1.45x — well above the industry average, which is below 1.2x. Some of the stronger names in the space have much lower leverage: Gladstone Capital at 0.66x, Fidus at 0.73x, Main Street at 0.76x, Hercules at 0.89x, Ares Capital at 1.03x, and Trinity Capital (TRIN) at 1.06x.

Generally, the closer a BDC gets to the 2.00x leverage limit, the less attractive it looks to investors. That is because it has less room to borrow more, and it becomes more vulnerable to market swings or problems within its own portfolio.

This weakness was shown in yesterday’s Q1 2025 results. Monroe reported lower key figures, with the most disappointing being the 34% drop in adjusted Net Investment Income. Concerns are beginning to emerge around the sustainability of the payout, due to the fact that the company distributes a $0.25 per share dividend but only reported $0.19 in adjusted NII. The management highlighted that the quarterly dividend (paid on March 31) was covered by spillover income from previous strong performance and that the Company currently holds an estimated $0.53 per share in undistributed spillover income. As you can imagine, if the only interest thing about MRCC is a jeopardized dividend, the outlook isn’t great.

Out of Monroe’s total borrowings of $294 million at end 2024, 55% is tied to floating-rate revolving credit facilities, while the rest is in 4.7% fixed-rate notes maturing in 2026. This raises questions about whether Monroe will realistically be able to refinance at such favorable terms, a scenario that appears unlikely, making the road ahead even more challenging.

According to the Company, net asset value [NAV] fell to $186.9 for Q12025 ($8.63 per share) from $191.8 million ($8.85 per share) on December 31st. The investment portfolio was affected by unrealized losses attributed to certain portfolio companies, with the Q1 dividend exceeding the company’s net investment income for the quarter. Finally, according to CFO/CIO Mick Solimene, there were 10 non-accrual investments in the portfolio in Q4 2024, including some older, legacy assets that have been there for a long time. Among them are two cases tied to legal disputes, which are in the process of being resolved. The amounts involved are being held in escrow accounts or similar arrangements until things are settled.

Conclusion

To be honest, I do not see any reason to invest in Monroe, as both the company's fundamentals and the stock’s behavior justify my “Hold” recommendation, while yesterday’s financial metrics confirm its weakness.

The $1 per share dividend (around 14% dividend yield) is noteworthy, but it is not enough to offset the capital losses resulting from the stock’s persistent downward-sloping trend.

The financial results are weak, especially when compared to sector averages, and I am not confident that the dividend is fully covered.

Even for investors seeking to diversify into “second-tier” BDCs, MRCC is not an option. Despite its low valuation, I do not believe it is worth the risk.

data.title: Church & Dwight Co.: Increasingly Difficult To Justify Valuation Premium
data.description:
Summary

I gave a hold rating to Church & Dwight Co. (CHD) previously, with my key thesis being that slowing domestic growth would likely trigger a de-rating in multiples. My current view after looking at the 1Q25 results is to remain hold-rated. The weak domestic performance, compounded by retailer destocking and new tariff headwinds, validates my cautious stance. In my view, the challenges are real, making the path to reaccelerating growth and justifying its current multiple increasingly difficult in the near term.

Earnings results update

CHD reported its 1Q25 numbers last week, and they were negative. Net sales came in at $1,467.1 million, a decline of 2.4% y/y, falling short of the company's own guidance. More concerning was the organic sales performance, which decreased 1.2% y/y and missed the low end of the guided 0-2% range. Digging deeper, this miss was entirely driven by a 1.4% volume decrease, which is even more worrying when management pointed to continued market share gains across many of its brands.

While CHD did manage to print $0.91 adj. EPS, which beat its own guidance, the beat was not as positive as it seems. Adj. gross margin contracted by 60 bps y/y to 45.1% as higher manufacturing costs and the impact of that lower volume offset the benefits seen from productivity initiatives and product mix improvements. Moreover, CHD cut marketing spend (down 10.1% y/y) and benefited from a lower tax rate.

This shows that the beat was not driven by organic means.

Slowing Categories and a Tentative Consumer Signal Trouble

I think what is becoming clearer is that the US consumer environment is weakening, and this is the real reason CHD’s results were weak. Management basically called this out as well during the call, they noted a step-down in category growth: from 2.5% in 2H24 to just 1.5% in 1Q25, and by March it had turned flat. This got even worse when in April, where category growth fell to -1%. The specific words that management used were “tentativeness of the US consumer,” and I think that’s the right way to frame it.

The macro data tells the same story. In April, the Conference Board’s Expectations Index dropped to its lowest level since 2011 (this is basically in recession territory), and the University of Michigan’s consumer sentiment also fell sharply, with consumers becoming having more inflation concerns. What was notable here is that consumers specifically pointed to tariff headlines as the reason their inflation expectations jumped. And given that tariffs are unlikely to go away (this is my view) given how strong Trump has sounded on this, I believe demand will very likely stay weak for longer than expected.

Now, if you look at CHD’s portfolio, it is well spread across the value and premium spectrum, with brands Arm & Hammer and Xtra on one end, and brands like Therabreath, Hero, and Waterpik on the other. But in my view, this is not going to be enough to cushion the business in this kind of poor demand environment. 1Q25 already showed that broad-based category weakness is hitting CHD hard. What I worry about is the potential for the market to turn more competitive and promotional—because when demand softens everywhere, brands start fighting harder for share. This kind of setup tends to pressure margins, and while management did say they haven’t seen a big trade-down yet, which is reassuring, I think they are already facing pressure since they’ve started shifting their ad messaging to focus more on value. That suggests they’re preparing for more consumer pushback in the quarters ahead.

Retailer Destocking: A Surprise Headwind with No Expected Recovery

What makes things worse is that CHD is now getting hit by retailer inventory reductions. Management said this alone was a ~300bps drag on organic growth in 1Q25, which is a big number. To me that signals retailers are pulling back harder than expected, likely reacting to the weaker consumption trends and macro uncertainty. The bigger problem, in my opinion, wasn’t the size of the hit—it’s the change in management tone. In the past, I would’ve chalked this up to temporary timing—where retailers cut back for a quarter but eventually have to restock as sell-through catches up. But this time, management explicitly guided for no rebound in the rest of FY25. That tells us that they’re not expecting any quick rebound, and if I were to be blunt, it sounds like management had limited visibility. If retailers are planning to run lean on inventory for the rest of the year, CHD’s shipment volumes are going to stay under pressure. And with that, any hope for a near-term recovery in domestic growth starts to look a lot more distant.

Tariff Headwinds Add Another Layer of Complexity

On tariffs, it is a significant headwind. Management put the gross exposure at around $190 million on a 12-month basis—that’s roughly 17% of FY24 EBIT, so it’s not small. I will give management credit for moving quickly to mitigate the damage. They’ve exited or sold off businesses like Flawless, Spinbrush, and the Waterpik shower headline, which were most exposed, and they’ve also shifted some sourcing—Waterpik flossers, for instance, are no longer being brought in from China for the US market.

That said, even after all these moves, CHD is still exposed to ~$30 million to FY25 earnings. And this doesn’t even account for any potential retaliatory tariffs that might come through later. As such, while the response has been solid, the tariff drag is still material—and could get worse if the trade situation escalates.

Valuation

Overall, I was broadly right that a poor organic growth profile will drive a de-rating. Indeed, with the disappointing 1Q25 organic growth, the market de-rated CHD’s valuation (NTM PE) down to ~26x, below its historical average. And I think there is still more downside from here. Remember the peers’ comparison I did previously? Peers like Procter & Gamble (PG), Colgate-Palmolive (CL), and Clorox Company (CLX) now trade at an average of ~21.5x NTM PE with the same expected growth rate as CHD (previously CHD had a higher growth profile). I don’t see any strong reason why CHD should trade at a premium. I think the market will wake up to this if CHD prints another quarter of poor organic growth.

Conclusion

My view on CHD remains neutral. The significant guidance cut, driven by a weakening domestic consumer, retailer destocking, and new tariff pressures, confirms the challenges that CHD faces. The near-term path looks difficult with limited visibility on a growth re-acceleration. Given the now comparable growth outlook to peers but a still elevated valuation, the risk of further multiple compression remains high; hence, I'd rather be on the sidelines.

data.title: UFP Technologies: A Steady Performer
data.description:
In April, I called UFP Technologies (NASDAQ:UFPT) a medical sterile producer which looked quite clean in a premium article in my service. Following a 50% stock pullback, the custom medical component manufacturer looked quite appealing after an interesting M&A streak in 2024.

Trading at a multiple in line with the wider market, I liked the combination of M&A capabilities, organic growth and a defensive business model in an uncertain economic environment. The company just posted a solid first quarter earnings report, and while organic growth has slowed down, it was in part driven by one-offs, while leverage has come down nicely and room for further M&A is emerging.

This keeps me still upbeat on the business, even after a recovery from the April lows. I consider this an interesting investment for the remainder of the year.

Medical Custom Manufacturing

Founded back in 1963, UFP Technologies is an innovative designer and custom manufacturer of comprehensive solutions which are used for medical devices, sterile packaging and other engineered products.

Over time, the company has grown to a revenue base in excess of half a billion, with 4,000 workers providing these products across 20 manufacturing facilities. The company provides these services alongside a long process which starts with customer challenges, design & development, prototyping, tooling, custom equipment, pilot manufacturing and commercial production.

The focus on niche markets makes for high margin activities, added expertise, long-term and intimate customer relationships, and long-term manufacturing capabilities. 90% of the business is tied to the medtech business of the company, accompanied by modest other advanced components used in automotive, aerospace, defense and industrial applications. Within the medical segment, the business is very diversified between robotic surgery, interventional & surgical, surfaces, orthopedics, infection prevention and therapeutics, among others.

The company has seen strong growth over the past decade, as it combined organic growth with bolt-on M&A efforts. Over the past decade, revenues have quadrupled to just over half a billion, as the company has seen meaningful margin progress, with operating margins moving to the high-teens, all while very minimal dilution has been incurred.

This has resulted in a $20 stock in 2015 having seen solid gains, as it rose to the $100 mark in 2022. Momentum carried the stock away to a high of $360 in October 2024, after which shares were down to the $180s in April.

About The Valuation

At the start of 2024, UFP reported a 13% increase in 2023 sales to $400 million as GAAP operating profits of $58 million yielded margins in the mid-teens, with earnings seen around $6 per share.

After a 7% increase in first quarter organic sales, the company announced a very interesting and compelling deal for AJR. The company spent just $110 million to add $75 million in sales to the business, complemented by more smaller deals over the summer. This meant that reported growth and margins growth accelerated rapidly throughout the year, accompanied by decent organic growth.

The company reported a 42% increase in fourth quarter sales to $144 million, as organic growth was reported just shy of 7%, while operating profits were reported at $22 million. This resulted in adjusted earnings of $2.46 per share, trending around $10 per share. Net debt of $176 million came in around 1.5 times leverage based on quarterly EBITDA of $30 million being extrapolated.

Trading at 18-19 times earnings, while organic growth was still seen and leverage was manageable, I felt that appeal was seen, even as no 2025 guidance was provided, although the company sounded upbeat on the prospects. Furthermore, some of the deals only closed during the year, resulting in reported growth in 2025, even if organic growth would come in flat.

Much Anticipated First Quarter Results

Early in May, UFP reported its first quarter results, numbers which were much anticipated. First quarter sales rose by 41% to $148 million, as organic growth slowed down to 2.3%. Slower growth was attributed to a 15% fall in the advanced component business (the non-medical business) as well as inventory de-stocking within the medical segment, notably the robotics segments.

Operating profits rose in excess of topline sales growth, as operating profits of $23 million were sound, and adjusted earnings of $2.47 per share were up a penny on a sequential basis, while net debt fell to $169 million. This provides room for more dealmaking, as the company confirmed that it is already considering some opportunities at this point in time, including a potential larger deal.

Right now, the 7.8 million shares of the business have risen some 8% in response to the news, trading up to the $215 mark. At this level, equity is valued just shy of $1.7 billion, for an enterprise valuation just north of $1.8 billion. With revenues trending near $600 million per annum, that results in a reasonable 3 times sales multiple, with shares now trading at 21-22 times earnings.

Of course, organic growth was slowing in the quarter, but this was in part due to incidentals. Comments by management about tariff exposure were furthermore very comforting, as only $8 million of sales (just over 1% of total sales) are subject to 10% tariffs.

What Now?

The truth is that UFP has seen quite a solid quarter and, evidently, there seems to have been some valid reasons for the slowdown in organic growth, making me more comfortable that organic growth should return to mid-single digits in the coming quarter. Of course, there are risks to this, depending on how the economy will evolve.

In the meantime, shares have re-rated a bit since April, but now trading at 21-22 times earnings, multiples looks reasonable. This comes as the company is seeing leverage come down below 1.5 times, allowing for more room for M&A again. The company confirmed that it is looking to do some more deals.

Right now, I am a happy holder of the shares here, seeing more room for shares to expand. I am patiently awaiting the action later this year.

data.title: ATI Inc.: Takes Off After Q1 Beat, Yet Still Undervalued And Poised To Climb Further
data.description:
The Thesis

The ATI Inc. (NYSE:ATI) stock has been very volatile in the past couple of months, moving in a wide range due to uncertainties in the broader market, primarily due to recent tariff developments. However, the stock is still up in high single-digit percentages since my last bullish article, outperforming the S&P 500, thanks to the recent surge in stock price after solid Q1 2025 results.

I expect the strong growth momentum to continue further as the demand environment for the company's specialty material remains robust in the A&D end markets, which, along with elevated backlog, should drive volume and top line growth in the quarters ahead.

For margins, ATI's focus on operational improvements and its strong market positioning, with the majority of its production footprint located in the U.S., should be key growth drivers in the current uncertain environment due to tariffs. Overall, given the company's favorable growth prospects and a discounted valuation, I am maintaining my bullish view and would recommend buying this stock at the current levels.

Q1 2025 Highlights and Outlook

ATI’s Advanced Alloys segment (AA&S) has been under pressure in most of 2024 due to a soft demand environment in certain end markets. However, exiting the year, the segment delivered decent double-digit growth, thanks to strong shipments in the defense end market during the fourth quarter. This momentum continued further as ATI entered into 2025, which, along with continued strength in the High Performance Materials & Components (HPMC) segment due to solid jet engine sales, resulted in a year-over-year growth of approximately 9.7% across the consolidated top line, reaching $1.14 billion in the first quarter of 2025.

While the volumes remain strong across both segments, favorable pricing in the HPMC segment and strong operational performance across both segments benefited the company’s margin performance as its consolidated adjusted EBITDA margin expanded about 250 bps year-on-year to 17% in the first quarter of 2025.

Although the AA&S segment saw notable margin improvement during the quarter, this overall margin expansion was primarily driven by a 400 bps jump in the HPMC segment adjusted EBITDA margin, reaching a record 22.4%. Increased EBITDA also benefited the company’s bottom line performance as its adjusted EPS jumped to $0.72 from $0.48 in the prior year quarter, beating the consensus estimates by a notable $0.13 in Q1 2025.

After experiencing modest growth throughout 2024, the company entered 2025 with strong growth across the top line, showing strength across both segments. I expect the company to continue to benefit from a strong demand environment across the A&D end markets, especially in jet engines, which grew approximately 35% in the HPMC segment in the last quarter. While the demand remains robust, the backlog levels also remain elevated, providing solid near-term visibility. And as engine material order continues to show strength with no signs of cancellation, I believe the company’s overall volume should grow in the quarters ahead.

Apart from this, the demand for the company’s titanium capabilities also remains strong, as evidenced by a new contract from a leading airframe OEM, making ATI its leading supplier for flat products, which should significantly benefit the company’s top line in 2025 and beyond. The commercial aerospace end market is also expected to experience growth in the latter half of the year, which should further support overall volume in 2025. However, in the AA&S segment, the industrial market is expected to experience declines in the rest of 2025 due to challenging macros and weak electronic sales, partially offsetting the strong growth momentum in the rest of the business.

Overall, while weakness in certain parts of the businesses is expected to continue in the near term, a robust demand environment in the core A&D end market, particularly in the jet engines, and anticipated recovery in certain end markets in the quarters ahead should support top line growth in the near term. On the other hand, the company remains focused on shifting its mix toward high-growth A&D end markets to over 65% of total revenue. In my view, the ATI’s highly differentiated products and titanium capabilities should help it gain market share in this end market in the future and drive its consolidated top line growth in the coming years.

Valuation Update

I last covered ATI after its Q4 2024 results in February earlier this year, maintaining my Buy rating based on the company's strong margin and bottom-line performance and expectations for continued growth. However, the stock experienced a sharp decline of nearly 35%, hitting a new 52-week low near $39 in April after that article, due to a strong sell-off in the broader market amid market volatility over concerns around tariffs and trade policies. But, despite the sell-off, ATI stock experienced a quick rebound, gaining over 60% within a month, supported by solid Q1 2025 results, making a fresh high near $69. As a result, the stock is up about 9% since my previous article, outperforming the S&P 500 notably, which has been down about 8% during the same period.

Currently, the stock is trading at a forward non-GAAP P/E multiple of 22.75, based on its FY25 EPS estimate of $2.98. Despite the recent rally in the stock price, the valuation still looks attractive when compared to the historical average multiple of 44.53x, especially considering its double-digit earnings growth expectations for 2025.

I expect the demand environment for the company's products to remain strong, primarily in the A&D end market, which, along with support from backlog, should drive overall top line growth in the quarters ahead. While volumes are anticipated to grow, ATI's continued focus on improving operational efficiency and favorable pricing across most of the business should support margins in the quarter ahead.

It is also important to note that the recent tariff development is expected to put pressure on the company's cost structure in the near term However, I expect this to be minimal, thanks to the company's U.S.-based production for the majority of its material. Although there are certain materials for which availability remains an issue, and which need to be imported, the company's dependency on high-tariff locations like China is comparatively less due to its flexible and well-diversified supply chain. In my view, these factors should continue to support the company's margins and bottom line, further enhancing the stock valuation in the future.

Risks

Although I remain confident about ATI's solid growth momentum, certain risks persist that could weigh on the company's performance in the quarters ahead. As also discussed above, while demand remains healthy across most of the end markets, volumes are expected to decline on the industrial side due to weak order trends. Apart from this, ATI is expecting about $50 million in annual cost exposure from the recent tariff developments, which might put pressure on the margins despite disciplined cost management in the quarters ahead. In my view, these factors could impact the company's stock performance in the future.

Final Verdict

As discussed above, the ATI stock is still trading lower than its historical average multiple. Continued strong demand in aerospace and defense is set to drive the overall top line in the near term. Anticipated volume expansion, along with gains from positive pricing and better efficiency, are expected to benefit the margins. Given the strong growth trajectory in the quarters ahead, favorable long-term outlook, and reasonable valuation, I would like to reiterate my "BUY" rating on this stock at the current multiple for investors with a long-term view.

data.title: Six Degrees Of Integration: An Update On How Centers Of Platform Gravity Are Shaping The Cybersecurity Technology Market
data.description:
Introduction

About a year ago, we first articulated the “centers of gravity” around which we saw cybersecurity technology platforms gathering. The concept of a platform that consolidates multiple functionalities is hardly new in security. In a market characterized by fragmentation (more than 3,000 vendors participate in cybersecurity by our estimate), it has long been a product strategy that embraces consolidation.

While consolidation often serves a vendor's growth priorities through the addition (organically or by acquisition) of new or emerging functionalities, a platform strategy would not be successful if it did not answer customer needs first and foremost.

Much of the current attention given to cybersecurity platforms focuses on those serving the security operations center (SOC) in threat detection and response, but there are other focal segments around which security platforms have gathered.

These centers of gravity have crystallized much of today’s market momentum in the space, emphasizing the fact that there is no one-size-fits-all strategy for security platformization.

More recently, however, high-profile events uniting security for cloud native environments with threat detection and response highlight how some of these gravity centers are coming together with each other, with the intent of making a whole platform greater than the sum of its parts.

From consolidation of segments, to consolidation of platforms, we expect these trends in cybersecurity technology to elaborate further – particularly as the multiple impacts of generative AI become more pervasive throughout technology.

As a starting point for observing these trends, we first offered the following take on the six centers of platform gravity we identified last year. We reiterate those now to provide a baseline for more recent developments in the market, as well as context for where we see the platformization trend – and its noteworthy counter-trends – evolving. While platforms offer benefits for many, there will always remain a need to integrate other technologies into the enterprise security stack for a variety of reasons. We explore these trends as well, to give buyers a more comprehensive view of these market dynamics.

The take

The security market's fragmentation partly reflects how it evolved. Emerging adversary techniques and technological innovation have driven awareness of new security needs. This can introduce opportunities for new startups and product segments.

Capturing these new spaces is the objective of acquirers' organic and inorganic growth - and thus a major motivation for venture-backed plays. Consolidation helps reduce the complexity of a plethora of tools, offering a "single throat to choke."

However, platforms must go beyond consolidation, to ultimately integrate information and action across functionalities, reflecting the sequence of processes customers follow when pursuing a comprehensive security strategy.

In this post, we summarize six of these centers of platform gravity in cybersecurity, as well as additional areas we are watching for their impact on shaping the market. Synergies are evident, but platforms have their limits. They must also navigate the "paradox of success" by mitigating the concentrated risk introduced when consolidation is widely adopted.

The six

Across the cybersecurity landscape, certain focal points have crystallized platform trends. Others may yet follow, but today, six are having a notable impact on the evolution of the market (see figure). While not a strict depiction of synergies and overlaps, the diagram below suggests the many intersections.

Figure 1: Six centers of gravity for cybersecurity platforms

Source: S&P Global Market Intelligence 451 Research © 2025 S&P Global

Security operations, threat detection and response: One of cybersecurity's most familiar platform venues, SecOps represents the responsive side of security, where organizations monitor for threats, detect and correlate evidence of being targeted, and handle response activities such as asset protection and incident investigation.

Security information and event management (SIEM) has long been an anchor for this capability, centered largely on the collection of log data, and expanded with various approaches to anomaly and threat detection.

Recently, tools that gather telemetry directly from distributed sources such as endpoints and network systems have made threat detection and response more actionable at the point of contact with a threat. We have seen these platforms evolve to embrace multiple techniques in extended detection and response (XDR), disrupting legacy incumbents such as SIEM and giving rise to new security market leaders.

XDR platforms often incorporate log management and SIEM functionality to embrace not only this aspect of detection, but also correlation, threat hunting and historical analysis, as well as to support initiatives such as audit and compliance. SecOps platforms have been augmented further by workflow and automation tools to help security teams manage detection and response activities - with artificial intelligence playing a prominent role in the field's evolution.

Cyber exposure risk management: If SecOps platforms are the reactive side of security that responds to threats, cyber exposure management is the more proactive side of assessing risk posture and remediating exposures.

Its roots are in vulnerability management, long supported by the two pillars of assessment and remediation. Other functionalities that enable a more comprehensive awareness of exposure and asset inventory include attack surface management and third-party cyber risk management.

With potentially thousands of security vulnerabilities in any digital environment, the ability to prioritize remediation has become a focus. Although prioritization has long been an aspect of vulnerability management systems, newer risk-based vulnerability management vendors have emerged in response to this need, applying additional inputs such as active exploits in the wild, and other factors contributing to more effective prioritization. Increasingly, practical and evolved approaches to cyber risk measurement are shaping this space.

Security for cloud-native applications: With the proliferation of cloud-native assets, the need for protection and security telemetry specific to them has grown as well. A highly elastic cloud-native environment can scale at daunting speed.

As cloud-native security data has burgeoned, so has the need to assess exposures, mitigate cloud-specific threats, and manage access entitlements. This has led to providers building portfolios in cloud security posture management (CSPM), cloud identity and entitlements management (CIEM), and cloud detection and response (CDR).

While labels like CNAPP (cloud-native application protection platform) point to the overall platform theme, others distinguish between the proactive aspects of CSPM and the more reactive and operational aspects of a CNAPP portfolio.

Secure access service edge/security service edge: During the pandemic, demand for secure remote access increased substantially, considerably adding to the vendor opportunity for integrating private network access with the security functionality previously found primarily in corporate networks, extending the enterprise security investment to any accessible venue.

Capabilities may include more sophisticated approaches to network access control, such as zero-trust network access, software-defined wide-area networking, data loss prevention and secure web gateway.

Those with an emphasis on security for SaaS assets may offer functionality such as cloud access security broker capability or SaaS security posture management. Because this platform model lends itself to expansion based on changing secure corporate access needs, additional functionalities may be further incorporated.

Identity and access management: If the above centers of platform gravity are more operationally focused, they often have one thing in common: Access control is on the front line of both the proactive and responsive aspects of cybersecurity - as a key control, and often as a primary target of attack. This raises the significance of IAM as a center in its own right.

IAM systems have long had platform characteristics. It is not just a central resource integrated with multiple assets, but critical for operational availability throughout an environment. As demands on access control have evolved, vendors offering workforce IAM (WIAM) technology have expanded their range, incorporating techniques such as multifactor authentication, privileged access management, and identity governance and administration.

An emphasis on zero trust in implementing control aims to reduce exploitable gaps in traditional approaches. WIAM may interface with identity threat detection and response (ITDR) and identity security posture management (ISPM) for hardening user accounts against exploit, and detecting attempted or successful account takeover.

Distinctions between WIAM and IAM for business customers of digital services (customer IAM) may extend accounts into the millions, and prioritize reducing friction, completing transactions and integrating payments. Yet there is an actionable middle ground between workforce personnel and purely external entities.

Extending access to third parties such as contractors, partners and suppliers must be handled differently - and business-to-business IAM is an emerging aspect of IAM platform extensions.

Human-technology interaction: This is a more recent center for cybersecurity platforms, rooted in securing enterprises against one of the most active threat vectors: exploiting human susceptibility to manipulation at key points where humans interact directly with technology.

Malicious email, phishing, spam and other risky content targeting an organization's people is a primary example of such interaction – but so also is human interaction with web content in the browser.

While email security, anti-phishing and web-borne threats – as well as combinations of all of these – may be at the heart of this trend, protecting against malicious content is only half the battle. How people interact with it is another — and in some respects, is far more challenging.

Well-crafted attacks may appear to be completely legitimate and entirely benign. Security awareness training and other emerging techniques can make threat recognition more actionable, but enterprises must also recognize when human activity could introduce a threat.

This creates an opportunity to extend behavioral analytics to such a platform strategy, and it has factored into recent acquisitions. Visibility into the information that people and organizations reveal about themselves may play a role in recognizing and mitigating risk, introducing the potential for digital risk protection.

An evolving trend

Since we first published this research, we have seen the convergence of some of these centers of gravity. A particularly high-profile example is Google Cloud’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) record-breaking agreement to acquire Wiz for $32 billion in cash according to 451 Research’s M&A KnowledgeBase – the largest deal in cybersecurity to date.

Around this same time, we saw Palo Alto Networks (PANW) announce the re-branding of its cloud security portfolio to focus on synergies with its Cortex brand, which primarily targets threat detection and response for SecOps.

While common interests in cloud threat detection and response (CDR) highlight synergies between cloud security and SecOps platforms, having these centers of platform gravity as a reference point enables us to call out the role they play in shaping the market, when they become strong gravitational attractors driving convergence.

Given that security platforms have been evolving for decades, we expect that there will be further centers of gravity that may emerge. Vendors of data security technology, for example, are among the largest in the market.

Unlike other security platforms, pure-play data security vendors often have diffuse boundaries with built-in security controls factored into applications such as SaaS platforms. Yet here, too, we see trends like data security posture management, with a growing number advocating their approach as a "data security platform."

Vendors of application security technologies may offer a portfolio of tools, from software supply chain assurance to static and dynamic application security testing. While these tools may integrate functionalities, their use often occurs sequentially when invoked in development and deployment processes - and may play roles in the organizationally specific toolchains for DevSecOps.

There is also the question of the structure that platforms can take. In a "vertical" model, the platform would incorporate patterns seen in other centers of gravity, but specifically applied to the platform's focal segment.

An example would be identity, which has recently capitalized on the themes of posture management (and thus ISPM), threat detection and response (and thus ITDR), and attack surface management (and the resultant identity attack surface management).

Similarly, for CSPM and CDR, in a more "horizontal" model, a cyber exposure and risk management platform might assess exposure and risk across the digital landscape. However, in implementing controls to mitigate risk, capabilities in adjacent centers of platform gravity may be added, such as cloud-native applications or identity (both evident in CIEM).

The overlap between email security and the behavioral analytics applied in threat detection and response is also evident in the rise of this functionality in the human technology interaction plays from email security and anti-phishing providers.

The case of cyber exposure and risk management already illustrates some of the limits of platform reach. While these tools can highlight and prioritize the need for exposure remediation, deploying mitigation like patching is often more the responsibility of IT operations teams - although platform providers have added patching functionality to offer expanded options in seeing risk remediation through to completion.

Yet here, too, we see evidence of convergence. Threat detection and response, for example, may benefit from prioritizing attacks against known vulnerabilities and exposures in the environment.

Similarly, prioritizing limited resources for vulnerability and exposure remediation may be made more efficient by identifying exposures actively targeted, as may be evident in threat detection data.

Already we see platform vendors emphasizing these synergies in their portfolio – while innovative startups have also seized upon this opportunity, as was evident at industry events such as the RSAC Conference US held recently in San Francisco.

Identity, meanwhile, could have the most disruptive impact of all across this landscape. We expect non-human identities (NHI) in particular to be a force in the market in the coming months, given that NHIs may outnumber human identities in the enterprise by an order of magnitude.

This could be particularly relevant when it comes to associating identities with AI agents that can operate with at least some degree of autonomy. We expect that accountability for agent actions and the definition of specific access and activity privileges (and their limits) for agents to become a significant factor shaping the broader technology market in the not-too-distant future.

Counter-trends… and the paradox of success

Platforms, however, aren’t the only factor in play in cybersecurity. Indeed, much of the market’s fragmentation has come about because of opportunistic innovation in response to intelligent adversaries constantly in pursuit of new ways to circumvent defense.

So long as there is demand for innovation and new approaches in security, there will be room for startups, point products and pure plays. Indeed, many of today’s largest cyber tech vendors began as startups that disrupted the status quo.

This means there will always be a role for the integration of security technologies and data beyond a given vendor’s platform – and an avid market has emerged to serve this need. Vendors such as Query exist specifically to serve organizations that maintain multiple tools but have ample motivation to federate insights across those tools while leaving their technologies and data “in situ” for valid cost or productivity reasons, while Cribl and its competitors offer data integration systems specifically designed to serve security teams.

It's further worth noting that the reliable availability and consistency of data for AI implementations specifically intended to optimize security technology is a strong motivator for security data integration. Platform vendors themselves must embrace all these realities, too – and many of the best-informed among them offer extensibility and integration with third-party tools that serve the wide-ranging needs of their customers.

Other factors can limit the appeal of a platform play, such as customer concerns about the potential for vendor lock in. Ironically, this risk represents the paradox of platform of success. A global IT outage in 2024 attributable to an incident affecting CrowdStrike (CRWD) technology made clear that a widely adopted platform may concentrate risk when a failure affects critical dependencies.

This is far from affecting cybersecurity alone, however. The short-term fallout from this particular incident became evident shortly after its occurrence, but it remains to be seen whether future events will galvanize action to mitigate such risks or impact the long-term evolution of cybersecurity platforms that has been such a force up to now.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
